segments.s687-g9: اگرچه is a concessive conjunction ('although/even if'). شود is the literary third-person singular of شدن ('become') used here to state a hypothetical/result (often rendered as 'is/becomes' or 'may become'). بخت او is the noun phrase 'his fortune' (bakhth = luck/fate + pronoun او). دیرساز is a compound adjective/agent noun ('one that makes things late' = 'slow-coming' / 'delayer') that predicates بخت او; word order is slightly inverted for poetic effect (verb + predicate).
segments.s687-g10: The clause repeats the literary شود ('will become/comes') as main verb. بخت پیروز is a noun phrase where پیروز ('victorious') modifies بخت ('fortune'). با introduces accompaniment ('with' or 'accompanied by') and خوشنواز functions adjectivally/nominatively as 'sweet-sounding' or 'a pleasant player' (i.e., pleasant music); the whole clause asserts that a victorious fortune arrives with pleasant music.
segments.s687-g11: جهان‌آفرین ('world‑creator') is the subject (a divine epithet). برتن here is the fused prepositional phrase بر تن 'on the body / upon' (poetically rendered as a single token) and links to کیقباد as the person affected: 'upon Kay Qobad'. The phrase sets up the indirect object/beneficiary of the following verbs.
segments.s687-g12: ببخشید is the past simple of بخشیدن in the sense 'bestowed/gave' (the divine subject bestowed something upon Kay Qobad). و is the coordinating conjunction 'and'. گیتی is the direct object 'the world', بدو is the indirect object 'to him', باز is an adverb 'again/back', and داد is the past of دادن 'gave' — together: 'and (the) world he gave back to him.' Word order is the literary SOV/compound order with some inversion for emphasis.
segments.s687-g13: نماند is the negative/past-meaning form of ماندن here meaning 'remained / was left' (used idiomatically to say nothing was left). به بهرام marks the beneficiary/target 'for Bahram'. هم is an emphatic 'also/too'. تاج و تخت are the coordinated direct objects 'crown and throne'. The verb appears fronted for poetic emphasis, with the object phrases following.
segments.s687-g14: چه introduces an interrogative/exclamatory clause 'what...' and اندیشد is the literary third-person singular present/subjunctive of اندیشیدن ('would think' / 'might think'), used rhetorically. این مردم نیک‌بخت is the subject phrase 'these fortunate people' that follows the verb (inverted order for rhetorical effect).
segments.s687-g15: ز is the poetic preposition 'from/of'. دهقان نژاد is a genitive phrase meaning 'of peasant (or rural) stock' (nēzhād = lineage). ایچ ... مباد is an optative/negative formula: ایچ 'no/none' + مباد ('may it not be'), together meaning 'may there be no such people'. The whole segment expresses a wish that no one of peasant origin behave in the following way.
segments.s687-g16: که introduces a relative/subordinate clause 'who/that'. خیره here means 'dazed/astonished' and, with دهد ('gives/causes'), forms a causative-like expression 'causes (them) to be dazed' (دهد is the literary present/past root 'gives/causes'). خویشتن را is the reflexive object 'themselves' (را marks it as definite/direct object). رابباد is the fused token representing the idiomatic bādad/بباد construction meaning 'to be scattered/ruined/let be blown away' (i.e., 'ruin themselves / throw themselves away') — the clause describes people who, stupefied, consign themselves to ruin.
segments.s687-g97: چنین is an adverb meaning 'thus/so' that sets the manner; تا ... رسید is a temporal clause 'until/when he reached' with تا + past tense رسید (he reached). به here is the preposition meaning 'to', followed by پور سیاوش 'the son of Siavash' as the object of رسید.
segments.s687-g98: This clause is the result clause: زره 'armor' is the direct thing perceived; در برش is a locative phrase attached to the armor (literally 'on/around him' — here showing the armor was on the man), آشکارا is an adverb 'clearly/evidently', and بدید is the past tense 'saw'. Word order is typical Persian (object + locative + adverb + verb).
segments.s687-g99: بدو گفت is an older/perhaps poetic sequence meaning 'he said to him' (بدو = 'to him/unto him'); ای is the vocative 'O!', and the comparative phrase بتر از 'worse than' takes از plus the standard of comparison; خار گز is the noun phrase 'tamarisk thorn' (گز = tamarisk).
segments.s687-g100: به میدان is a dative/locative 'to the field / into the arena'; که introduces a relative clause 'who/that' (classical Persian uses که for relative links), and پوشد is the present/relative-form of پوشیدن 'to wear' describing the person; زره زیر خز is the noun phrase 'armor under (beneath) a fur', with زیر 'under' modifying زره and خز 'fur/velvet' as the covering.
segments.s687-g101: بگفت این is 'he said this' (بگفت = he said, این = this). The coordinating و links the speech to the action: شمشیر کین is a genitive-like compound 'sword of vengeance', and برکشید is the past tense 'he drew/raised (it)' — here used for drawing the sword.
segments.s687-g102: سراپای او is a possessive phrase 'his whole body / from head to foot' (سراپا used adverbially), پاک functions as an intensifying adverb 'completely/cleanly', and بر هم درید is a verbal phrase 'tore apart/ rent (him)'; the verb درید is past tense 'tore'.
segments.s687-g103: چوبندوی is a proper name or epithet (here the person who observes); زان 'from/of that' refers back to the killing just described, کشتن is the noun 'killing', آگاه شد is the compound 'became aware' (آگاه = aware, شد = became) in past tense.
segments.s687-g104: برو here functions as a narrative particle 'thereupon/then' (an archaic or poetic usage rather than the ordinary imperative 'go'); تابش روز 'the shining/radiance of the day' is the subject phrase, and کوتاه شد is 'became short' — a verbless change-of-state construction with شد as the auxiliary 'became'.
segments.s687-g49: This phrase gives the target, instrument and location in classical Persian order: به + proper name (به زهراب) marks the target/recipient (here 'at/against Zahrāb'), شمشیر is the instrument ('a sword') and در بزمگاه is the locative ('in the banquet‑hall'). Word order is prepositional phrase → noun → locative, typical of Ferdowsi's diction.
segments.s687-g50: بکوشش is a verbal/imperative-subjunctive-like form 'strive/endeavor'; توانمش is a fused form meaning 'I am able (it/ him)' (توانم + -ش referring to the object), کردن is the light verb 'to do/ to make/ to cause', and تباه is an adjective 'ruined'. Together they form an intentional construction: 'I will endeavor (بکوشش) that I may be able (توانمش) to make (کردن) [him] ruined (تباه)'.
segments.s687-g51: بدریای آب is a possessive/genitive phrase 'Badrīyā's water(s)'; اندرون is the locative 'inside/within'; نم نماند is a negated past-form meaning 'no moisture remained' (نم = moisture/dew, نماند = did not remain). The clause thus states a complete absence of moisture within Badrīyā's waters.
segments.s687-g52: که introduces the result/explanatory clause ('so that' / 'such that'). بهرام را uses the object/topic marker را but here functions to highlight Bahram as the one concerned; شاه is the predicate noun 'king'; بایست is a modal 'must/ought'; خواند (here) means 'to call/proclaim'. The sequence 'شاه بایست خواند' is a classical construction meaning 'must be called/proclaimed king.'
segments.s687-g53: بدو is a prepositional pronoun 'to him'; گفت is the verb 'said' (here used as the reporting verb); بندوی is the subject 'the bondman/servant'; کای is a vocative particle 'O' addressing someone; کاردان is an epithet 'skilled, expert'. The sequence places the indirect object ('to him') and the reporting verb before the subject and the vocative, a word order found in classical epic style.
segments.s687-g54: A string of coordinated adjectives modifying the addressee: خردمند 'wise', و 'and', بیدار 'awake/alert', و 'and', بسیاردان 'very learned' (بسیار + دان). The coordination is simple and typical: multiple qualities enumerated with و.
segments.s687-g55: بدین زودی is an adverbial phrase 'in this shortness' = 'soon' (بدین = به + این, archaic/poetic form). اندر is a locative/prepositional element 'into/in', and جهاندار شاه is the compound title 'world‑holder king' (the sovereign). Together they set the time ('soon') and name the subject ('the sovereign king').
segments.s687-g56: بیاید is an optative/present-subjunctive form often used to express future action 'will/shall come'; نشیند is the present-subjunctive 'sit down' (here coordinated with بیاید to mean 'come and sit'); برین is 'بر + این' ('upon/at this'), and پیشگاه is 'presence/court'. The two verbs form a classical sequence meaning 'he will come and sit in this presence.'
segments.s687-g89: ندانم is the negative present/future‑oriented clause 'I do not know' with دانـ as the root. که introduces a subordinate content clause ('that/what ...'). در دل is a prepositional phrase 'in (the) heart' with usual word order (preposition + noun). چه دارد is an embedded interrogative 'what he has'; دارد is the present tense 'has/holds'. ز بد attaches afterward in poetic order as a genitive/causal phrase 'of/from bad' (= 'of ill/evil'), modifying what he holds.
segments.s687-g90: This clause gives an admonition. تو is the 2nd‑person subject 'you'. ز + او is written here across two items (ز then و) and functions as the preposition + pronoun 'from/of him' ('away from him'); in classical/poetic orthography the pronoun can attach, hence the split. خویشتن دور داری is literally 'keep/hold yourself distant' (داری = present 'you have/hold' used with دور to mean 'keep (yourself) away'). سزد is an impersonal present 'it befits/it is proper'.
segments.s687-g91: چو بــشنید is a temporal clause: چو = 'when' and بشنید is the past tense 'he heard'. چوبینه modifies گفتار as an adjective (literally 'wooden' or 'club‑like') giving a figurative quality to 'speech'. گفتار زن = 'the woman's speech/words' (noun + genitive).
segments.s687-g92: که introduces reported content ('that / namely that'). با او means 'to/with him' (با = with/by, او = him). همی‌گفت is the iterative/habitual past 'kept saying' (the progressive 'was saying' in older Persian). چوگان مزن is a quoted imperative: چوگان = 'the polo‑mallet' (instrument) and مَزن here is the prohibitive imperative 'do not strike' (negative command).
segments.s687-g93: هرآنکس که is a free relative 'whoever / anyone who'. رفتی is a verbal form here marking the action 'went/enters' (classical narrative uses forms that may look different from modern colloquial). به میدان اوی is a prepositional phrase 'into/onto his field' (میدان = arena/field; اوی is a possessive form 'his').
segments.s687-g94: چو here again = 'when'. نزدیک گشتی is a verbal phrase meaning 'you draw near / you approach' (gشتی is an older verbal form denoting the change/state of becoming near). بچوگان = with the mallet (ب‑ prefix = 'with' + چوگان 'mallet'), و = 'and', گوی = 'ball' (sphere) used here as the object of play. The line is elliptical; the clause sets up an encounter when one approaches with mallet and ball.
segments.s687-g95: زدی is a past/perfective verb 'you struck' or 'you hit/placed (your) hand'; دست بر پشت is the direct action 'hand upon the back' (preposition بر = on/upon). اونرم نرم is a doubled adjective construction: اونرم (≈ 'that soft' / 'his soft') + نرم 'soft/gentle', emphasizing the gentleness of the action (poetic doubleness).
segments.s687-g96: سخن گفتن is a noun+infinitive phrase 'to speak / speaking words' (here rendered as a description 'speaking words'). خوب qualifies گفتن as 'pleasant/well', و links to آواز گرم ('and a warm voice'). آواز گرم means a fervent or warm tone; the adjective گرم qualifies آواز and indicates warmth/fervor rather than literal temperature.
segments.s687-g153: This is a noun phrase listing locale: بیابان (wilderness) + بی‌راه (pathless/off-road) joined by و (and) to جای دده (the elder's place). The order is descriptive: broad locale (wilderness, pathless) followed by a more specific site (the elder's place).
segments.s687-g154: سراپرده‌ای is an indefinite object ('an inner curtain/tent'); دید is the past tense verb 'he saw'; جایی زده is a participial/adjectival phrase modifying the tent — زده (past participle) here indicates 'set up/pitched' (i.e. located there). The noun plus participle follows normal Persian pattern where the participle comes after the noun it qualifies.
segments.s687-g155: نگه کرد is a past tense light-verb construction meaning 'he looked/kept watch' (نگه + کرد). موسیل (Mosul) followed by بود ارمنی is a copular clause: بود is past 'was' and ارمنی is the predicate 'Armenian' (i.e. the place/person was Armenian); the clause is inserted after the verb as a parenthetical remark.
segments.s687-g156: هم ... هم is the correlative 'both ... and'. آب روان is a compound meaning 'flowing water' (آب = water; روان = flowing), یافت is past 'found', and خوردنی here is a substantive/adjective meaning 'edible / things to eat'. Word order: [both water] یافت [and food], with یافت as the main verb applying to both items.
segments.s687-g157: جهان جوی is a compound noun 'world-seeking' (جهان = world; جوی = seeker/searcher). بندوی (devotee/bondman) is an appositive or epithet; تنها is an adverb 'alone'. برفت is the past tense verb 'went off' (archaic/past form of رفت). This is a compact noun phrase + verb: 'the world-seeking devotee went alone.'
segments.s687-g158: سوی خیمه‌ها is a prepositional phrase 'toward the tents' (سوی = toward; خیمه‌ها = tents). روی بنهاد is a light-verb construction meaning 'he set/put his face' i.e. 'he turned his face/eyes toward' (روی = face; بنهاد = placed/put, past). تفت is a verbal noun/verb meaning 'to roast/scorch' and here indicates the action he set about (likely preparing food). The clause thus reads 'he turned toward the tents and began to roast/prepare.'
segments.s687-g159: چو is the conjunction 'when'. مو سیل here functions as the proper name 'Mosil' (written in two parts in the manuscript); را marks the following noun as the definite object of دید ('saw'). بردش نماز is a verb phrase where بردش = برد + enclitic -ش 'he took him' (he led/took him) and نماز is the destination/object 'to prayer' — together 'he took him to prayer.' Note: بردش is a composite with the object suffix attached.
segments.s687-g160: بگفتند is a past plural 'they spoke/said' (here 'they conversed'). با او is the prepositional phrase 'with him'. زمانی دراز is a temporal phrase 'a long time' (زمانی = a while/time; دراز = long) with adjective after the noun, a common poetic order.
segments.s687-g145: نکشتیم is the past 1st-person-plural form with a negative prefix (ن-) meaning “we did not kill.” The verb precedes its object (common V–O order in Persian poetry); بندوی را uses را as the definite direct-object marker attached to the name Banduy. از نخست is an adverbial phrase “at first / initially” qualifying the action.
segments.s687-g146: ز دستم رها شد is a passive-like construction: ز (= از) + دستم “from my hand/in my power,” رها شد “was released/escaped” (رها as verb + شد auxiliary). در چاره جست is a compact clause meaning “sought a remedy/way out,” where جست is the past of جستن used here as “sought.”
segments.s687-g147: برین is the fused phrase بر + این meaning “about/for this.” کرده خویش is “his/one’s deed” (کرده = deed/done, خویش = his/one's own, here split orthographically into خوی + ش). باید گریست is an impersonal modal: باید “one must/it is necessary” + گریست (infinitive/past-stem used for the sense “to lament/weep”), giving “one must lament.”
segments.s687-g148: ببینیم is 1st-person-plural jussive/subjunctive “let us see / we shall see.” تا introduces a subordinate/purpose clause here meaning “so that / to find out.” رای یزدان is a genitive phrase “the counsel/plan of God.” بچیست is an archaic interrogative-assertive form meaning “what is it?” (i.e. “what is God’s counsel?”).
segments.s687-g149: وزان is the fused classical particle از + آن, “from that / thereupon / for that reason.” روی here participates in the adverbial phrase (وزان روی) giving a sense of 'on that account' or 'thereupon.' بندوی و اندک سپاه is the coordinated subject: “Banduy and a small army/force.”
segments.s687-g150: چوباد is the literary compound چو باد “like the wind” (simile). دمان here (classical دَمان/دامان) is literal “skirt/hem” used idiomatically with بر گرفتند راه to mean they gathered up their skirts and set off; بر گرفتند is a verbal phrase “took up / seized” (گرفتند) with the prepositional/ directional بر, and راه is “the road / the way.” The whole clause describes a swift departure.
segments.s687-g151: همی‌برد is an imperfective/habitual past form “was carrying / kept carrying / carried off.” هرکس که introduces a free-relative “whoever / everyone who,” and بد بردنی is a descriptive phrase: بردنی is a participial/adjectival form meaning “that can be carried/portable,” while بد here is ambiguous in the manuscript (classical uses vary); the whole phrase means roughly “whatever was carry-able” or “what could be taken.”
segments.s687-g152: به راهی is a directional phrase “toward / onto a road.” که موسیل بود is a relative clause “that was (leading to) Mosul” (موسیل = Mosul, بود = was). ارمنی is an adjectival tag “Armenian,” here specifying the region/character of the route (i.e. Mosul in the Armenian lands).
segments.s687-g121: پشیمان شد is a two-word predicate: پشیمان (an adjective 'regretful') + شد (past of شدن 'to become'), so it means 'became regretful'. از + verbal noun کشتن marks the cause ('because of/killing'); Persian commonly uses the verbal noun after از to indicate the reason. یار خویش is a noun + possessive: یار 'companion' followed by خوی + enclitic ش (here split in the data) giving 'his companion' (i.e. 'his own companion').
segments.s687-g122: کزان is a connective 'from which / from that' referring back to the previous clause. تیره دانست is adjective + verb: تیره 'dark/ominous' and دانست (past of دانستن used here = 'deemed/considered'), so 'considered ... dark'. بازار خویش is a possessive NP 'his market/marketplace' (in poetry can mean 'his fortunes/affairs'); word order is adjective + verb + object (he considered his market dark).
segments.s687-g123: چنین گفت is a fixed phrase 'thus he said' (چنین = 'thus/so'). کآنکس (archaic) introduces a relative/indefinite 'the one who / whoever'; که is the relative particle 'who/that'. دشمن ز دوست uses the poetic preposition ز ('from') in the contrastive expression 'enemy from friend' = 'enemy from/versus friend' — the clause awaits the verb نداند in the next segment: 'who cannot tell enemy from friend'.
segments.s687-g124: نداند is the present/past-classical negative form of دانستن here 'does not know / cannot tell'. مبادا is an optative/wish particle used in curses and prayers ('may it not be' / 'may ...'); here it introduces a hostile wish. ورا = 'to him' (object pronoun), followed by the noun phrase مغز و پوست 'brain and skin' (object). The clause is elliptical — a violent wish is implied ('may his brain and skin be taken/stripped'), which is typical in poetic curses where the destructive verb may be omitted.
segments.s687-g125: یکی is an enumerator 'one (of them)'. خفته is a past participle/adjectival form meaning 'asleep' or 'lying (dead/asleep)'. بر is the preposition 'on/upon'. تیغ دندان پیل is a genitive chain: تیغ (blade/edge) qualified by دندان پیل ('the elephant's tooth/tusk'), so together 'on the edge/blade of the elephant's tusk.'
segments.s687-g126: Again یکی 'one'. ایمن is an adjective 'safe, protected (from)'; Persian omits the copula, so ایمن از ... reads '(one) is safe from ...'. از marks the source 'from' and موج دریای نیل is 'the waves of the sea/river Nile' (دریای here = 'waters/sea of'). The clause contrasts safety from natural danger with the previous fate.
segments.s687-g127: دگر 'another' introduces a different person. آنک is an archaic relative particle meaning 'that one who / he who'. بر پادشا شد دلیر is 'became brave before the king': بر + noun can indicate 'in front of / toward' in older Persian, شد is past of شدن 'became', and دلیر is the predicative adjective 'brave'.
segments.s687-g128: چهارم 'the fourth' numbers this man among others. که is the relative 'who/that'. بگرفت is the classical past form with the prefix b- (بگرفت = 'seized, took hold of'). بازوی شیر is a genitive 'the lion's arm' (بازو 'arm' with شیر 'lion'), the object of بگرفت. Together: 'the fourth who seized the lion's arm.'
segments.s687-g65: گشاده شود is a passive/impersonal verbal phrase ('become opened, be revealed') with شود = 'become'. زین is the archaic contracted preposition 'از این' (= 'from/through this') and syntactically introduces the source phrase زین سخن. راز تو is a genitive phrase 'your secret' (راز + emphatic/poetic pronoun تو). Word order places the source phrase before the head 'راز' for poetic emphasis.
segments.s687-g66: بگوش آیدش is an idiomatic sequence: بگوش 'to the ear' + آید (comes) with the clitic -ش = 'to him/it', so literally 'it comes to his ear' = 'he hears'. روشن modifies آواز ('a clear/distinct voice') and تو is the possessor 'your' following the noun in this poetic order (آوازِ تو).
segments.s687-g67: چو introduces a temporal clause 'when'. بشنید is the simple past 'he heard' (here the verb of the subordinate clause). بهرام is the subject, and شد + تازه روی is a resultive phrase 'became fresh-faced / his face brightened' — شد is 'became' and تازه روی is a compound adjective 'fresh-faced / renewed countenance'.
segments.s687-g68: هم اندر زمان functions as an adverbial 'at the same time / simultaneously'. بند برداشت is a past transitive clause: بند (fetter, bond) is the direct object and برداشت is the past 'lifted/took away'. زوی is an archaic postpositional/clitic form meaning 'from him / from it', marking the source from which the bond was removed.
segments.s687-g69: چو روشن شد is a temporal clause 'when ... became bright/illuminated' (شد = 'became'). آن چادر مشک رنگ is a noun phrase 'that musk‑colored tent': آن = 'that', چادر = 'tent/veil', مشک رنگ is a compound postpositive adjective 'musk‑colored' (adjective after the noun in poetic order).
segments.s687-g70: سپیده is personified subject 'Dawn'. بدو is the dative pronoun 'to it/unto that (tent)'. اندر آویخت is the past 'hung upon' (اندر = 'upon/in', آویخت = 'hung'), and چنگ is the direct object 'harp'. Word order places the dative before the verb for poetic effect.
segments.s687-g71: ببندوی is a fused form equivalent to ببند وی = 'bind him' (imperative/optative); here rendered as a command in reported speech. گفت is the reporting verb 'he said' (positioned after the command in the line). ار is the archaic conditional 'if'. دلم is دل + possessive clitic -م 'my heart' and نشکند is the negative-subjunctive/present form of زدن/شکستن 'to break' (ن + شکند) meaning 'be not broken / will not break' depending on context.
segments.s687-g72: چو here introduces a temporal clause 'when'. چوبینه is an adjective/noun 'wooden' (or 'the wooden [one]') qualifying چوگان 'polo‑mallet', and امروز is the adverb 'today'. زند is the present/subjunctive of زدن 'to strike / to hit' (third person singular), so the clause reads 'when the wooden mallet strikes today.'
segments.s687-g1: همی‌بود is an archaic continuous/past marker (همی‑ + بود) meaning 'was/kept being'. بندوی is a compound/poetic noun 'bondman, captive'. بسته is a past participle/adjective 'bound, tied' that follows the noun. چو introduces a simile ('like'), and یوز names the animal 'cheetah' — so the whole chunk says he continued to be a bound captive, like a cheetah.
segments.s687-g2: به + noun gives the place/target: به زندان بهرام = 'in/into Bahram's prison' (زندان + بهرام = genitive/name phrase). هفتاد روز is a duration placed after the place phrase: 'for seventy days.'
segments.s687-g3: Word order is noun + modifier: نگهبان بندوی = 'the guard of the bondman/prisoner.' بود is the past copula 'was.' The whole segment states the identity of the guard: Bahram was the prisoner's guard.
segments.s687-g4: کزان is a relative/prepositional particle 'from/through whom' referring back to Bahram. بند او = 'his bond/fetter' (بند = imprisonment/fetter, او = his). نیک is an intensifying adverb 'well/very' and ناکام here carries the sense 'thwarting/unsuccessful' — together نیک ناکام بود says his fetter did not allow success (i.e. attempts at freeing him were frustrated). بود is the copula 'was.'
segments.s687-g5: ورا = 'to him' (object pronoun referring to the captive). نیز = 'also/likewise.' بندوی again 'the bondman/prisoner.' بفریفتی is an archaic narrative verb form (بـ + past stem + ending) meaning 'deceived/enticed' (a past action in the epic register).
segments.s687-g6: ببند (root ببند) = 'bind/close' (here 'shut up'); اندر = 'in/within'; از چاره = 'of/for a remedy/means' (چاره = way out, solution). نشکیفتی is a poetic/old form meaning effectively 'no solution came/found' (negative + verbal idea) — together the clause says they shut him inside so that he found no way out.
segments.s687-g7: که introduces an exhortation/explanatory clause here ('so' / 'therefore'). از شاه ایران مشو ناامید is a negative imperative: از ... مشو = 'do not become/of (lose hope) concerning the Shah of Iran' — i.e. 'do not despair of the King of Iran.' مشو is the negative imperative 'do not become' and ناامید is the state 'hopeless.'
segments.s687-g8: Standard conditional: اگر = 'if'; تیره شد روز = 'the day becomes dark' (تیره = dark, شد = became); گردد سپید = 'it will turn white/bright' (گردد = becomes/turns, سپید = white/bright). This is proverbial: even if times grow dark, they will grow bright again.
segments.s687-g137: اگر introduces a conditional clause ('if'). چشمه is the thing desired ('a spring'); خواهی is the 2nd-person present/future-form of 'to want' ('you want'). که introduces the content clause ('that'), and بینی is 2nd-person present of 'to see' ('you see'). بچشم here is a verbal stem constructed with the prefix بـ- plus چش- (from چشیدن/چشیدن in older usage meaning 'to experience/feel/see'); together بچشم شوی will link into the following predicate. Word order is conditional clause → complement clause → verbal complement.
segments.s687-g138: شوی is 2nd-person singular present/subjunctive of شدن 'to become' ('you will become'). خیره is an adjective meaning 'dazed/staring/astonished' and functions as the predicate complement. ز is the poetic preposition 'from/of'; و here functions as a small connective ('then/so' or introducing the next clause). بازگردی is 2nd-person present/subjunctive of بازگشتن 'to return/turn back' and بخشم is 1st-person singular present/future of بخشیدن used here in the sense 'I forgive' or 'I will pardon' — the sequence gives two consequences: being dazzled, and (if) turning back, forgiveness follows.
segments.s687-g139: کسی is an indefinite pronoun 'anyone/someone'. راکجا is an interrogative adverbial phrase 'to/at what place; where' (classical order often places the interrogative after the pronoun: 'کسی را کجا' = 'where would anyone...'). کور بد رهنمون is a noun phrase with two participial/adjectival modifiers: کور 'blind' and بد 'bad' modifying رهنمون 'guide'. The phrase functions as a descriptive nominal group ('a blind, bad guide').
segments.s687-g140: بماند is 3rd-person singular present/subjunctive (or optative) of ماندن 'to remain' used here in a hypothetical/rhetorical sense ('would remain'). به introduces the prepositional phrase marking direction/place ('on/toward'), followed by راه 'way/path' modified by دراز 'long' and the noun اندرون 'within/inside' — together 'on the long way within'. The verb precedes its locative phrase, as is common in classical Persian.
segments.s687-g141: هرآنکس که is a relative/indefinite clause beginning 'whoever/anyone who'. گیرد is 3rd-person singular present of گرفتن 'to seize/take', and بدست literally 'in the hand' marks the manner ('seizes with the hand'). اژدها is the object 'the dragon'. The verb phrase follows the relativizer 'که' in typical classical order.
segments.s687-g142: شد + کشته forms a passive/resultative: شد (became/happened) + کشته (slain) = 'was slain'. او is the patient 'he' (the person who grabbed). The conjunction و links the next clause. اژدها ز (از او) رها: the poetic preposition ز = 'from' plus رها 'free' (here predicate 'was freed' or 'escaped'), so 'and the dragon was freed from him' (i.e. the dragon got free of that person). Word order is predicate after subject as in classical Persian.
segments.s687-g143: وگر is the conditional 'if' (classical 'but if'). آزمون را uses the object marker را after آزمون to topicalize 'the trial/ordeal' (i.e. 'in the trial'); کسی is 'someone/anyone'. خورد is 3rd-person past/present of خوردن 'to eat/take' but here idiomatically 'to take/receive' (i.e. 'ingest' or 'suffer'), and زهر is 'poison'. The clause means 'if someone in the ordeal takes the poison.'
segments.s687-g144: ازان 'from that' (= 'from that fact/act'). خوردنش is a gerund/nominalized form 'his eating/its being eaten' (خورد + نش suffix = 'the act of eating/receiving it'); درد و مرگست is a verbless/existential predication 'there is pain and death' (مرگست = 'it is death' or 'is deadly'). بهر at the end is a classical prepositional/adverbial particle meaning 'for' or 'to the detriment/for him' — so the whole means 'from that act of swallowing comes pain and death for him.'
segments.s687-g113: زمیدان (here written fused) is the prepositional phrase z-e meydān 'from the field/battlefield' placed before the clause for emphasis; چو is the subordinating conjunction 'when'; بهرام is the subject; بیرون is an adverb 'out/away'. کشید is the past tense verb 'drew/pulled' placed at the end in the classical word order. Together: 'When Bahram drew out from the field...'.
segments.s687-g114: همی is the poetic verbal particle (here functioning with the final verb) that gives the action a vivid narrative force; دامن 'hem/skirt' is the (grammatical) object placed before the verb; ازخشم 'out of/from anger' is an adverbial phrase explaining motive; در خون 'in blood' is another adverbial phrase describing result; کشید is the final past tense verb 'drew/dragged/stained' (classical word order: adverbials and object precede the verb).
segments.s687-g115: ازان پس is a temporal sequence 'after that/thereupon' (ازان + پس). بفرمود is an honorific-past tense verb 'he commanded/ordered/said' (used of kings). مهروی را is the direct object (a personal name or title) marked by را. The whole clause: 'Thereafter he ordered Mahruy...'.
segments.s687-g116: که introduces a reported subordinate clause 'that/so that'; باشد is the classical jussive/subjunctive 'may be/let him be' (indicating the content of the order); نگهدار is a predicate noun 'guardian/keeper'; بندوی is the beneficiary/possessed noun 'bondman/servant'; را is the object/topic marker attached to بندوی to mark it as the one to be guarded. Grammatically: 'that he be guardian of the bondman.'
segments.s687-g117: ببهرام is the prepositional phrase be + Bahram 'to Bahram' (the dative), here fused; گفتند is the reporting verb 'they said' (classical past); کای is the vocative particle 'O'; شهریار is the addressee 'king'. The clause functions as 'They said to Bahram, "O king..."'.
segments.s687-g118: دلت را = دل + second-person possessive -ت + را (your heart, marked). ببندوی here should be read as the prepositional phrase به بندوی 'to/for the bondman' (written fused in the text), followed by رنجه مدار, a negative jussive idiom meaning 'do not grieve/afflict' (رنجه 'be distressed' + مدار used to form 'do not do' in classical poetic commands). The whole line is an admonition: 'Do not distress your heart for a bondman.'
segments.s687-g119: که is the subordinating conjunction 'when/that'; اوچون is an archaic fused expression (here functioning like 'thus/as soon as' or linking adverbially) — the exact sub-senses are archaic; از این کشتن = 'of/from this killing' (از + این + کشتن); آگاه شد is a two-word predicate 'became aware' (آگاه 'aware' + شد 'became' as past auxiliary). Entire clause: 'When he became aware of this killing...'.
segments.s687-g120: همانا is an emphatic particle 'verily/truly'; که introduces the ensuing statement; با باد همراه شد is a short phrase 'became accompanied with/by the wind' — همراه there describes accompaniment ('together/with' or 'attendant'), and شد is the past 'became/went'. In context it means he departed swiftly 'like the wind.'
segments.s687-g161: 'بدو گفت' is a compact expression: 'بدو' is the prepositional/clitic form 'to him/unto him' placed before the verb, and 'گفت' is the past tense 'said' (subject implied earlier). The three following items (موسیل زایدر مرو) are a bare sequence of names/places presented in apposition or vocative style (no conjunction), i.e. a list of destinations being addressed.
segments.s687-g162: که introduces a subordinate clause with a causal/‘for’ value ('for/because'). The clause has the subject 'آگاهی' (information/news) followed by the verb 'آید' ('comes'); the pronoun 'تو' appears with the object marker 'را' (تو را) to mark the person affected — word order here is inverted compared with modern prose (verb before the clitic object). 'نوبنو' is an adverbial/qualifier meaning 'anew/afresh' modifying the coming of the news.
segments.s687-g163: که here introduces a content (indirect) clause: 'در روم آباد' is a prepositional phrase 'in (the) flourishing/prosperous Rome' where آباد postmodifies روم. 'خسرو' is the agent (Khosrow) and 'چه کرد' is an interrogative/relative sequence meaning 'what (he) did' — together: 'what Khosrow did in prosperous Rome'.
segments.s687-g164: همی is an adverb giving a continuous or emphatic sense ('would/ever/indeed'); 'آشتی نو' is the noun phrase 'a new reconciliation/peace' (noun + adjective). 'کند' is the third-person singular verb 'would make' (classical subjunctive/optative/habitual sense). The enclitic 'گر' is the conditional 'if' attached after the verb; 'نبرد' (literally 'battle') completes the conditional idea: 'he would make a new peace if he had not fought' (classical word order places the conditional particle and the conditional clause tightly with the verb).
segments.s687-g165: چو is a temporal conjunction meaning “when” (introducing a subordinate temporal clause). بشنید is 3rd-person singular past (“he heard”); in classical Persian the verb often precedes the subject. بندوی is the subject here (a compounded form meaning “his bondman/servant” or “that bondman”) placed after the verb. آنجا بماند is the result clause: آنجا “there” (locative adverb) + بماند, 3rd-person past/ narrative form “he stayed” (classical/persian epic usage keeps the verbal prefix بـ and -د ending).
segments.s687-g166: وزان is و + زان meaning “and from that” (a connective prepositional phrase). دشت is the noun “plain” (the location being referred to). یاران خود را is the object phrase: یاران “companions” with خود as a reflexive/possessive “his own,” and the object marker را (written attached here as رابخواند). رابخواند is the 3rd-person past “he summoned/called”; in poetry orthography the را is fused to the verb.
segments.s687-g41: "مگر کو" here functions as a rhetorical interrogative — roughly "could it be/is there someone who..."; the clause that follows names the thing and recipient: "به نزد تو انگشتری" literally "a ring to your presence" = "a ring to you/for you." Word order places the destination phrase (به نزد تو) before the noun انگشتری, which will be completed by the verb in the next segment.
segments.s687-g42: فرستد is a third‑person verb form (classical/subjunctive/optative sense) meaning "send/might send; will send." همان is an emphatic demonstrative "that same/that very," and the following noun phrase افسر مهتری identifies the agent ("the chief officer"). Together this yields "that very chief officer sends (or would send)."
segments.s687-g43: چوـبشنید is the fused classical pattern meaning "when/on hearing." The following words give the experiencer and what was heard: بهرام is the subject and سوگند او is the object phrase "his oath." In this construction the temporal clause precedes the main verb in the next segment.
segments.s687-g44: بدید is the simple past "he saw." آن دل‌پاک is a demonstrative + adjective noun phrase "that pure‑hearted (one)" and و پیوند او adds a coordinated noun "and his bond/loyalty." The pronoun او at the end refers back to the person whose oath was heard, so the clause says he perceived that person's purity of heart and fidelity.
segments.s687-g45: بدو گفت is the idiom "he said to him" (بدو = به او). کاکنون = "that/now" introduces the speaker's intention. همه راز خویش is the object phrase "all (the) secrets of his/his own" (خویش = "his own"), but the main verb of disclosure (بگویم) appears in the next segment, so this segment sets up the direct speech content.
segments.s687-g46: بگویم is a first‑person present/subjunctive used to express the speaker's intention "I will tell/I shall tell." بر افرازم is a verbal phrase where بر + افرازم (from افراز/افراخت) gives the sense "I will raise/exalt/bring up," and آواز خویش "my voice/my cry" is the thing being raised — idiomatically "I will speak out about my case." The clitic ش on خویش marks the possessive.
segments.s687-g47: بسازم is first‑person present/subjunctive (classically used for an intended future) "I will make/prepare." یکی modifies دام as "one/a certain" and دام چوبینه را is a definite object phrase "a wooden trap" (را marks the object). The sequence states the speaker's plan to construct a trap.
segments.s687-g48: This clause states the intended result: بچاره = "the poor fellow/poor wretch" (the person who will suffer), followed by فراز آورم, a verb phrase where آورم "I bring/causes" combines with فراز (lit. "up/high") to mean "I will bring up/bring down upon" — i.e. cause to befall; کینه را is the definite object "the grudge/hatred" (را marks it). Altogether: "I will bring the grudge upon the poor wretch."
segments.s687-g57: تودانی (تو دانی) is an archaic/poetic two-word form meaning 'you know' (to + present 2nd sing. دانی). که is the complementizer 'that' introducing the content clause. من هرچ گویم is a subordinate clause 'whatever I say' with من as subject, هرچ 'whatever', and گویم the 1st-person present subjunctive/indicative stem used here as 'I say' (classical style). بدوی is an enclitic/old-form prepositional phrase meaning 'to him / toward him' (poetic order: verb or particle may follow the clause) and links this clause to the following verb نپیچد.
segments.s687-g58: نپیچد is the negative jussive/subjunctive (ن‑ + پیچد) meaning 'may (he) not turn/turn aside' or 'let (him) not pervert'. The subject (the king/he) is omitted and understood from context. ز here is the poetic 'from/of', گفتار 'speech/words', این بنده 'this servant' (humble self-reference), and روی is 'face' (used idiomatically as 'turning of the face / attention'); together they express 'may he not turn away from the words of this humble servant.'
segments.s687-g59: بخواهم is a first-person present/future sense 'I wish / I ask' (classical usage). گناهی 'a sin' is the object; که introduces a relative clause 'which', and رفت (past stem of رفتن) here means 'went / passed' (i.e., 'that passed/occurred earlier'). از تو پیش 'from you before/in your presence' places that sin as something associated with or done by you earlier. The whole segment expresses the speaker's desire regarding a sin of the addressee that occurred previously.
segments.s687-g60: ببخشد is a jussive/optative 3rd-person form meaning either 'may he forgive' or 'may he grant/bestow' (both meanings exist; here the context suggests pardon or granting). به گفتار من is an instrumental phrase 'by / through my speech/words', where به marks the means and گفتار من 'my words'. تاج خویش is 'his crown' (تاج = crown; خویش = his own, here split into خوی + ش in the word list), the object that may be given or restored. The clause thus asks that, by the speaker's words, the sovereign forgive or bestow his crown.
segments.s687-g61: اگر is the conditional 'if'. خود 'yourself' (emphatic). برآنی here is a classical/poetic form meaning 'you accept / you take to heart' (the literal senses supplied did not list a verbal sense, so this is an idiomatic reading); that is followed by که + گویی همی, 'that you say' (گویی used as 'you say' and همی as the auxiliary/old verbal particle marking continuous or emphatic aspect). The whole phrase sets a conditional: 'If you yourself accept/feel that what you say (is true)...'.
segments.s687-g62: به دل رای literally 'to the heart's counsel' (به + دل = 'in/to the heart'; رای = 'plan/opinion/counsel'), کژی 'crookedness/wrongness', نجویی is a present/subjunctive verbal form from جستن/جوییدن meaning 'seek' or 'look for' (here 'do not seek' is implied by the context), and همی is the classical verbal particle supporting the present. The clause admonishes the addressee not to look for crooked counsel within his heart.
segments.s687-g63: ز بند این دو پای من آزاد کن is an imperative: کن (do!) is the 2nd-person imperative 'free', with آزاد 'free' as adjectival complement. ز بند = 'from the bond/fetter', این دو پای من = 'these two feet of mine' (a poetic way to mean 'my two feet' = 'my two limbs' or 'my captivity'), so the speaker commands 'free my two feet from this bond.' Word order is poetic but straightforward imperative + object + prepositional source.
segments.s687-g64: نخستین 'first/firstly' is an adverbial, ز خسرو 'concerning/from Khosrow' (here 'remember Khosrow' or 'bring Khosrow to mind/regard'), برین is the fused poetic form of بر + این ('about this' / 'on this'), and یادکن is the imperative 'remember/mention.' Together: 'First of all, remember Khosrow about this' or 'first call Khosrow's attention to this.'
segments.s687-g81: زنی بود = 'there was a woman' (زنی noun + بود past 'was'). «بهرام یل را» follows and identifies whose woman she is — بهرام یل is apposition ('Bahram, the hero') and را is the object/definiteness marker used here in a possessive/relational sense (poetic word order places the possessor after). «نه پاک» is negation (نه before adjective پاک) giving 'not chaste.'
segments.s687-g82: که introduces a relative clause 'who/that'. «بهرام را» is the direct object marked by را. «خواستی» is a past/desiderative meaning 'desired/wished' (classical poetic ending), and «زیر خاک» is a prepositional phrase 'under the earth' placed after the verb, so the clause reads 'who wished Bahram under the earth' (i.e. wanted him dead).
segments.s687-g83: به دل = 'in/into the heart' (prepositional use). «دوست بهرام» is a genitive phrase 'Bahram's beloved/favourite' (دوست = beloved here). «چوبینه بود» describes the heart as 'wooden' or hardened (چوبینه 'wooden') with بود marking past state.
segments.s687-g84: که introduces explanation/reason. «از شوی» here reads 'since becoming (a wife)' or 'from the time of marriage' (شوی is a deverbal/derivative use meaning 'becoming a spouse' in this context). «جانش پر از کینه بود» = 'her life/soul was full of rancour' (جانش = her life / inner self; پر از + noun = 'full of'). بود is past copula.
segments.s687-g85: فرستاد is simple past 'he sent.' «نزدیک بهرام» is an adverbial phrase meaning 'near Bahram' (نزدیک before the name), and کس is an indefinite 'someone' (the person he sent). Word order places the adverbial between verb and complement for emphasis.
segments.s687-g86: که introduces the purpose/relative clause. «تن را نگه‌دار» is an instruction-like clause 'keep/guard the body' — را marks تن as definite object; نگه‌دار here functions as a verbal imperative/command 'hold/guard' (in poetry the same form can look nominal). «و فریاد رس» is a compound: فریاد 'a cry' + رس (reach/come) meaning 'one who comes in response to a cry' — i.e. 'and come quickly when a shout is raised' or 'and be a responder to cries.'
segments.s687-g87: که again introduces a following clause. «بهرام پوشید پنهان زره» = 'Bahram put on hidden armor': پوشید is past tense 'put on/wore' and پنهان qualifies زره ('hidden armor'). Poetic inversion places adjective/attribute before or after as convenient.
segments.s687-g88: برافگند is past 'threw off' / 'cast away' (here used of undoing or casting aside). The following phrase «بند زره را گره» names the object: بند = 'band/strap/bond', زره را marks 'the armor' as definite object of that band, and گره = 'knot.' Together the line means he loosed/removed the armor's strap knot (poetic word order places the modifiers around the verb).
segments.s687-g25: Here is a nominal phrase followed by a finite verb: 'یکی سخت سوگند' = 'one/one single severe oath' (numeric + adjective + noun). 'خواهم' is the 1st-person auxiliary/finite verb (archaic/persianate future/volitional) meaning 'I will / I shall (swear)'. 'بماه' is a fused prepositional phrase (به + ماه) meaning 'by the moon' — the preposition attaches directly to the noun in the poetic orthography. Word order (numeric/adjective → noun → verb → prepositional phrase) is typical of Ferdowsi's elevated style.
segments.s687-g26: This continues the oath's objects introduced by the preposition 'به' (here 'by/according to'). 'آذرگشسپ' is a proper name (a mythic figure) being sworn by. 'بتخت' is really 'به تخت' collapsed in poetic spelling and should be read 'by the throne'; the listed lemma is irregular, so treat it as the noun 'تخت' with prefixed preposition. The conjunction 'و' links items; 'کلاه' (literally 'hat', here 'crown') is the final item of the oath.
segments.s687-g27: 'که‌گر' is the fused conditional 'که اگر' = 'if'. 'خسرو' is the subject (here the king/name). 'آید' is the 3rd singular present/subjunctive 'comes' (classical/poetic form). 'برین' is 'بر' + demonstrative 'ین' = 'to/on this'; 'مرز وبوم' = 'border and homeland' (مرز = border; وبوم is a fused form meaning 'and the homeland'). The word order places the place-expression after the verb as is common in poetry.
segments.s687-g28: The clause 'سپاه آرد' is subject + verb: 'he/they will bring an army' (آرد is 3rd singular present/future in classical Persian). 'از پیش' here functions as a compound prepositional phrase meaning 'in the presence of / before'. 'قیصر ز روم' is a genitival phrase where 'قیصر' (Caesar) is qualified by 'ز روم' ('from Rome' — the poetic preposition 'ز' after the noun). Poetic word order places these modifiers after the verb.
segments.s687-g29: This is a conditional-address phrase: 'به خواهی' = 'if you wish' (به + خواهی, the 2nd-person present/future of خواستن used in classical style). 'م...را' is the object 'مرا' (me) split in the tokenization: 'م' + 'را' (را is the direct-object marker). 'زو' is written as 'ز' + 'و' here and means 'from him' (ز = from; و is the enclitic pronoun representing او). 'به جان زینهار' is a conventional oath/exhortation: 'by my life, take heed' — زینهار is an imperative 'beware/take heed.'
segments.s687-g30: 'نگیری' is the negative-subjunctive/imperative form (ن + گیری) meaning 'do not take/undertake' addressed to 'تو' (you). The object phrase 'این کار' = 'this deed/task' follows, modified by the adjectives 'دشوار' (difficult) and 'خوار' (humiliating/despised). The sequence gives a prohibition plus a descriptive result: 'do not take on this difficult, humiliating task.'
segments.s687-g31: 'ازو' = 'from him' (prepositional source). 'بر تن من' = 'upon my body' (بر + تن + enclitic pronoun من). 'نیاید' is the classical negative/future form 'will not come' (3rd singular), so the clause reads literally 'harm will not come upon my body from him.' 'زیان' here is the patient/subject 'harm' placed at the end as the noun being negated by 'نیاید.'
segments.s687-g32: 'نگردد' (from گشتن/گردیدن) is a classical 3rd-person negative/future meaning 'it will not become / it will not happen.' 'به گفتار' here uses 'به' in the sense 'in/regarding (speech)' — 'in the speech of...' — and 'ایرانیان' is the agent/arena 'the Iranians.' Together: 'it shall not become (a thing) in the speech of the Iranians' = 'people will not speak of it among the Iranians.'
segments.s687-g105: بپوشید is the past (3rd person singular) of پوشیدن with an initial narrative ب‑; here it means “he put on.” پس is a simple temporal adverb “then.” جوشن is a cuirass/armor (direct object). و برنشست joins the clause: برنشست is past (3rd sg.) of برنشستن “to sit upon / to rise (and sit/mount),” meaning “and he sat up / mounted.”
segments.s687-g106: میان is the preposition “among / in the midst of.” یلی is an attributive adjective “valiant, mighty.” لرزلرزان is a doubled adjective meaning “trembling / agitated” (poetic intensification). ببست is the past (3rd sg.) of بستن; literally “he bound/tied/fastened,” here idiomatically meaning he fixed/took his station among them. Word order (preposition + modifiers + verb) is compressed poetically.
segments.s687-g107: ابا here functions as a proper name (subject) in context (the provided lemma senses do not include a name, so treated as -1). چند تن is a phrase “a few men / some persons” indicating accompaniment; the preposition با is omitted poetically. رفت is past (3rd sg.) “went / set out.” لرزان is a participle/adjective “trembling” modifying the manner, and به راه is the directional phrase “onto the road.”
segments.s687-g108: گریزان شد is a two-word predicate: گریزان (fleeing) plus شد (past of شدن) = “became fleeing / took to flight.” از introduces the cause here (از = “because of / from”) and بیم means “fear.” بهرامشاه is the agent of fear (King Bahram). Grammatically this is a resultative construction: they became fugitives because of fear of Bahram-shah.
segments.s687-g109: گرفت is past (3rd sg.) “took / seized.” او is the pronoun “he.” ازان is an archaic/poetic contraction of از آن (“from that / out of that”), marking source. شهر is “city.” راه گریز is a noun phrase “the road/path of escape” functioning as the thing taken. Word order is somewhat compressed for poetic effect but the verb governs the following objects.
segments.s687-g110: بدان here is the fused form (به + آن) meaning “to/unto that” or “thereby”; it points to the destination/reference just mentioned. تا introduces purpose or a negative-purpose clause (“so that / lest”); نبینند is the negative present-subjunctive (3rd pl.) “they may not see.” ازو = “from him” (source), and رستخیز means “uprising / insurrection” (here: a rising coming from him). The clause expresses purpose/avoidance: he took the route so that they would not witness an uprising from him.
segments.s687-g111: به منزل رسیدند is a common narrative phrase “they arrived at a lodging/stop/stage” (رسیدند past 3rd pl.). و connects clauses. بفزود is a verb meaning “added / increased” (here: increased the number) and خیل is the object “host / crowd.” The same plural subject carries across the coordinated verbs (they arrived and increased the host).
segments.s687-g112: گرفتند is past (3rd pl.) “they took / seized.” تازان is the agent noun here meaning “the Tazans / raiders” (poetic name for fierce fighters). ره اردبیل is the route phrase “the road toward Ardabil” (the preposition to/into is elided for brevity). Together: “the raiders took the road to Ardabil.”
segments.s687-g33: بگفت این (He said this) — بگفت is a 3rd-person past-tense verb 'he said' with the subject omitted (typical in Persian). این is the demonstrative 'this' and is the direct content of what was said. و پس is a short discourse sequence 'and then' (و = and; پس = then), and دفتر زند خواست is a compact clause where دفتر ('notebook/register') is the object of خواست ('wanted/asked for') and زند qualifies دفتر (here 'Zend', i.e. the register/commentary). خواست is 3rd-person past 'he wanted/asked'.
segments.s687-g34: به سوگند بندوی رابند خواست (he wished to bind the bondman by an oath) — به سوگند is an instrumental/measure phrase 'by an oath' (به used as 'by/-ly'). بندوی is the patient 'the bondman/servant'; رابند should be read as را + بند ('bind the ...'), i.e. a verbal idea 'to bind' with the object marker attached before the verb-stem; خواست is again 3rd-person past 'he desired/wished'. The whole phrase functions as the complement of خواست: 'he wanted to bind the bondman by oath.'
segments.s687-g35: چو بندوی بگرفت استا و زند (When the bondman seized [him], he stood fast, and Zend...) — چو introduces a temporal clause 'when'. بندوی بگرفت is a simple clause 'the bondman seized' (بگرفت = took/seized). استا is an archaic/stylized past form meaning 'stood/held fast' (equivalent to ایستاد). و links to the following noun/agent zinc; زند here follows as a coordinated element ('and Zend' or 'and the Zend/commentary/reciter'), so the clause describes two consecutive actions/agents.
segments.s687-g36: چنین گفت کز کردگار بلند (thus he said that/from the lofty Creator...) — چنین گفت is the formula 'he said thus' (چنین = 'thus, so'; گفت = 'said' here used as the main speech verb). کز is a contracted/kest form meaning either 'that' or 'from which'; here it introduces the following phrase 'from the exalted Creator' (کردگار بلند), where کردگار = 'the Creator/God' and بلند = 'lofty/exalted' (adjective after the noun, a common classical order). The clause is the beginning of a quoted or reported statement.
segments.s687-g37: مبیناد بندوی جز درد ورنج (May the bondman see nothing but pain and grief) — مبیناد is an optative/jussive-form verb 'may it appear / may it be seen' (here functioning like 'may it be the case that…'). بندوی is the experiencer/indirect subject 'for the bondman'. جز introduces the exception 'except/only'; درد ورنج is a coordinated noun phrase 'pain and sorrow' (درد = physical or general pain, ورنج = grief/sorrow). The resulting meaning: 'may it appear to the bondman only pain and sorrow.'
segments.s687-g38: مباد ایمن اندر سرای سپنج (May he not be safe within the house of Sepang) — مباد is a negative optative 'may it not be', expressing a wish that follows the previous clause. ایمن = 'safe'; اندر = 'in/within'; سرای normally 'house/place' (here 'the house of ...') and سپنج is a proper/name or place-word following it. Grammatically this is a short optative clause wishing the opposite of safety for the bondman in that location.
segments.s687-g39: که آنگه که خسرو بیاید زجای (that when Khosrow comes from his place) — the first که is a subordinating/complementizer 'that'; آنگه adds 'then' or 'in that case'; the second که introduces the temporal subordinate 'when'. خسرو is the proper name 'Khosrow'. بیاید here is a 3rd-person present/subjunctive often used with future sense 'comes/shall come'. زجای is a prepositional phrase 'from his place' (ز = from + جای = place).
segments.s687-g40: ببینم من او را نشینم ز پای (I will see him and sit at his feet) — ببینم is 1st-person singular present/jussive 'I see / I will see' (here future-oriented), with من added for emphasis. او را is the object 'him' (را = definite object marker). نشینم is 1st-person present/jussive 'I sit / I will seat (myself or him)' — in Persian sequence of verbs with the same subject carries two planned actions: 'I will see him and I will sit.' ز پای is a locative/instrumental phrase 'at/from the foot(s)' (= 'at his feet'). The clause expresses the speaker's intended actions upon Khosrow's arrival.
segments.s687-g73: سگالیده‌ام is a past first-person form meaning 'I set dogs on / I hunted (with dogs)', the enclitic -ام marks 1st person. دوش is a temporal adverb 'last night' that follows the verb phrase. با پنج یار is a prepositional phrase 'with five companions' (با = with; یار = companions) modifying سگالیده‌ام.
segments.s687-g74: که introduces a purpose clause here ('so that'); از is the simple poetic preposition 'from'. تارک is an archaic/poetic noun whose exact sense in this line is uncertain (see senses). او is the 3rd-person pronoun 'him'. برآرمم is a subjunctive-like/purpose form 'that I may raise' or 'let me raise' (the extra -م marks the 1st person in an old poetic subjunctive). دمار is the object meaning 'ruin/devastation', the result intended by the purpose clause.
segments.s687-g75: چوشد is the poetic fusion of چو شد meaning 'when it became' and functions as the temporal conjunction 'when.' روز بهرام = 'the day of Bahram' (روز = day; بهرام = Bahram). چوبینه (literally 'wooden' / 'club') with روی ('face' / 'one of appearance') here forms an epithet (roughly 'club‑bearing' / 'club‑faced') modifying the subject; word order places the temporal clause first then the descriptive epithet.
segments.s687-g76: به میدان نهاد uses به + میدان to mark direction/place 'into the field/arena' and نهاد is the past of نهادن 'placed / set' (here intransitive sense 'entered / took to'). و is the coordinating 'and'. بچوگان and گوی are coordinated nouns 'the mallet (چوگان) and the ball (گوی)'; the verb that governs them is ellipted/understood from نهاد (i.e. he took/placed himself in the field with mallet and ball).
segments.s687-g77: فرستاده آمد is a simple past clause 'a messenger came' (فرستاده = messenger/sent one; آمد = came). ز is the poetic preposition 'from' and links the messenger's origin to بهرام. زود is an adverb 'quickly/soon' modifying آمد and placed after the clause for emphasis.
segments.s687-g78: به نزدیک here means 'to the vicinity/near the presence of' (به = to; نزدیک = near). پور سیاوش is an epic patronymic 'Pour (son of) Siavash' (پور functioning as 'son of' before the name). چودود follows the name as an epithet or gloss; its precise meaning is obscure in this line.
segments.s687-g79: زره خواست و پوشید is two coordinated past actions: 'he requested/asked for armor' (زره = armor, خواست = he wanted/asked) and then 'he put it on' (پوشید = he donned/covered himself). زیرقبای is an adverbial/location phrase 'under the robe' that specifies where he put the armor; word order places this locative after the verb.
segments.s687-g80: ز is the poetic preposition 'from' (here marking movement from the threshold). درگاه means the royal court/threshold/presence and functions as the place name. باسپ is an archaic/obscure word here (see senses) that appears in the prepositional phrase; اندر آورد پای is literally 'brought in foot' — اندر آورد (آورد) is the past 'brought/placed/put into' and پای is 'foot', so together the phrase means 'he set/placed his foot inside' (i.e. he stepped into the court).
segments.s687-g17: This is a compact imperative/adverbial phrase. بشمر is an imperative/hortative 'count' (2nd-person command or jussive), کنون is the temporal adverb 'now', تا دوماه gives the extent 'up to/till two months', and بانگشت supplies manner ('with a cry' / 'aloud' or 'calling out as you count'). Word order places the manner/voice word first for emphasis: 'aloud, count now until two months.'
segments.s687-g18: که introduces a subordinate/explanatory clause ('for/that'). از روم marks the source 'from Rome', بینی is 2nd-person present/subjunctive meaning 'you see' or future 'you will see', and به ایران سپاه (inverted order) means 'an army toward/into Iran' (سپاه is the noun, به ایران the goal). The clause as a whole: 'that you will see an army from Rome come into Iran.'
segments.s687-g19: بدین is an adverbial phrase 'by/with this' (classical بيت-form 'بهٔ این'), followed by the objects تاج و تخت 'crown and throne'. آتش functions as the instrument/what is cast, and اندرزنند is a 3rd-person plural present/subjunctive 'they set on/they attack' (classical verb meaning 'to lay upon, to attack'). The verb comes at the end and governs the preceding phrase.
segments.s687-g20: همه 'all' is the subject/agent; ز یورش uses the ablative/causal 'from/because of the onslaught' (ز marking source), بر سرش 'upon his head' gives the target (with ش = his), and بشکنند is 3rd-person plural subjunctive 'they will break / they will shatter'. The clause describes the effect of the assault: everyone will break (break him) upon his head.
segments.s687-g21: بدو is the prepositional/indirect-object phrase 'to him'. گفت is the past tense 'said' (here appearing with the indirect object before it; the listed lemma senses do not show the verbal sense), and بهرام‌گر شهریار is a vocative/appellative: the name 'Bahramgar' followed by شهریار 'king' addressing him. Word order places the addressee before the verb and the vocative after it.
segments.s687-g22: This clause contains a classical inversion: مرا (here split in the tokens as م + را) is the object 'me' (را is the object marker), داد خواهد is a future periphrasis meaning 'will give/hand over' (داد = give/hand over, خواهد = will), and به جان زینهار is an oath/interjectional phrase 'by my life — beware!' (به جان = 'by the soul/life', زینهار = 'beware/take heed'). The object appears before the verb for emphasis, and the warning oath follows.
segments.s687-g23: زپند is an ablative/agentive phrase 'by/from your counsel/advice'. توآرایش is a compound 'تو + آرایش' here functioning as 'your advice/array' (possessive), جان کنم is a verb phrase 'I will make (it) life' i.e. 'I will devote my life' or 'I will give my life (to it)'. کنم is first-person singular present/future 'I do / I will do'.
segments.s687-g24: همه 'entirely/altogether' modifies the clause; هرچ گویی 'whatever you say' uses هرچ 'whatever' plus گویی (classical 2nd-person subjunctive/optative 'you say' or 'you might say') to introduce a free relative; توفرمان is 'to/at your command' (توفرمان = 'your command' in medieval fusion), and کنم 'I will do' closes the sentence in the 1st-person promise. The clause therefore means 'I will do everything you order.'
segments.s687-g129: ببخشای is an archaic/hortatory verb form placed first: here it functions like a jussive/optative “may he spare/grant.” برجان is a fixed phrase meaning “for the life of” (an emphatic oath-like prepositional phrase). این هر چهار is a demonstrative + distributive: این (“these/this”) + هر (“each/every”) = “each of these four.” Word order is verb — adjunct — noun phrase, typical of poetic Persian.
segments.s687-g130: کزیشان is the relative/compound particle (کز = که + ز) referring back to the four; here it has the nuance “from them / of whom.” بپیچد is a classical 3rd-person verbal form of پیچیدن with the b- prefix (jussive/subjunctive/optative feel) meaning “may/does turn/twist.” سر روزگار is a metaphorical compound “the head of (one’s) time/fate” = “Fate” or “turn of fortune.” The whole segment is a relative clause modifying the four.
segments.s687-g131: دگر here means “another/again” and precedes the clause for emphasis. هرک is an indefinite-relative “whoever.” جنباند is the transitive verb (classical past/pret. stem) “moves/sets in motion.” او کوه را = “that mountain” (او as demonstrative + کوه ‘mountain’ with the object marker را). The clause reads “Again, whoever moves that mountain…”
segments.s687-g132: بران is an adverbial/locative particle “thereupon/thereon/for that,” linking to the place or result. یارگر is a noun “companion/helper.” خواهد is the present/future auxiliary “will/desires” (third-person singular). انبوه را (with را marking the definite object) = “a multitude/host.” Together: “will desire to gather/bring a host of companions there.”
segments.s687-g133: تن خویشتن را is the emphatic object “his own body” (تن + emphatic/reflexive خویِشتن, with object marker را). بدان رنجه داشت is a verbal phrase: بدان = بِ + دان (classical/poetic combining form ‘with that’), رنجه داشتن = “to afflict/bring distress.” داشت is past (“he had/kept”), so the phrase means “he afflicted his body with that suffering.”
segments.s687-g134: وزان = و + زان = “and/from that.” رنج تن = “the body’s suffering” (noun + possessor). باد here is the classical particle often used to mark a strong wish/optative or an intensifying/evidential flavor; in this line it functions idiomatically within the phrase باد در پنجه داشت = “had (it) in his grip.” در پنجه داشت is literal “had (it) in the claw/grip.” Overall: “and from that suffering he had the body (or its pain) in his grasp.”
segments.s687-g135: بکشتی is an instrumental/adverbial prepositional phrase “by ship / in a ship” (classical b- + کشتی). ویران modifies کشتی: “a ruined/shattered ship.” گذشتن is the infinitive “to pass/cross,” and برآب (“on/over the water”) is the usual complement for crossing water. The phrase as a whole means “to cross the water in a wrecked ship.”
segments.s687-g136: به آید is a common classical phrasing meaning “it comes to pass / one finds.” که introduces the content clause “that …”. بر کارکردن is the prepositional phrase “for/to the doing of the work” (بر + infinitive کارکردن), and شتاب is the noun “haste/hurry.” Together: “one finds that one must hurry to do the work.”
